sections.title: Group A Verbs (-ω)
sections.explanation: Group A verbs are the most common verb group in Greek. Their infinitive-like form (dictionary form) ends in -ω. To conjugate them in the present tense, remove -ω and add the personal endings: εγώ -ω, εσύ -εις, αυτός/ή/ό -ει, εμείς -ουμε, εσείς -ετε, αυτοί/ές/ά -ουν. Example: γράφω (to write) → stem: γράφ- → εγώ γράφω, εσύ γράφεις, αυτός γράφει, εμείς γράφουμε, εσείς γράφετε, αυτοί γράφουν. Other common Group A verbs: μιλώ (to speak) — note this can also behave as a contract verb, τρώω (to eat) — slightly irregular but follows Group A endings, πίνω (to drink), διαβάζω (to read/study), ακούω (to hear/listen). The subject pronouns are often omitted in Greek because the verb ending already indicates the person.
sections.tip: The endings -ω, -εις, -ει, -ουμε, -ετε, -ουν are the backbone of Group A. Notice that -ουμε (we) is unstressed — the stress stays on the stem (γράφουμε, πίνουμε). Don't confuse this with Group B's -ούμε, which is stressed on the ending.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'γράφω' (to write) for 'εσύ'?
exercises.explanation: The εσύ (you) ending for Group A verbs is -εις: γράφ + εις = γράφεις.

exercises.prompt: How do you conjugate 'πίνω' (to drink) for 'εμείς'?
exercises.explanation: The εμείς (we) ending for Group A verbs is -ουμε: πίν + ουμε = πίνουμε (stress stays on the stem).

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'ακούω' (to hear/listen) for 'αυτοί'?
exercises.explanation: The αυτοί/ές/ά (they) ending for Group A verbs is -ουν: ακού + ουν = ακούν (the ω drops). Αυτοί ακούν μουσική.

sections.title: Group B Contract Verbs (-ώ / -άω)
sections.explanation: Group B verbs are contract verbs whose dictionary form ends in -ώ or -άω (both spellings are accepted). They have different personal endings from Group A. The endings are: εγώ -ώ/-άω, εσύ -άς, αυτός/ή/ό -ά, εμείς -άμε, εσείς -άτε, αυτοί/ές/ά -άνε/-ούν. Example: αγαπώ (to love) → εγώ αγαπώ, εσύ αγαπάς, αυτός αγαπά, εμείς αγαπάμε, εσείς αγαπάτε, αυτοί αγαπάνε. Other common Group B verbs: περνώ (to pass/spend time), μιλώ (to speak — contracted form), ρωτώ (to ask), πετώ (to fly/throw), χτυπώ (to hit/knock). Notice that the stress always falls on the final syllable in Group B verbs, which distinguishes them from Group A.
sections.tip: All Group B endings carry the vowel 'α' (except the εγώ form). The stress on every form falls on the last syllable. If you hear a present-tense verb with final stress throughout, it is most likely Group B.

examples.en: He is having a good time.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'μιλώ' (to speak) for 'εσύ'?
exercises.explanation: Group B verbs use -άς for εσύ: μιλ + άς = μιλάς. Do not confuse with Group A which uses -εις.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'He passes / he is having a good time' using 'περνώ'?
exercises.explanation: The αυτός/ή/ό ending for Group B verbs is -ά: περν + ά = περνά.

exercises.prompt: Which ending is used for 'εμείς' with Group B verbs?
exercises.explanation: Group B verbs use -άμε for εμείς (e.g., αγαπάμε, μιλάμε), while Group A uses -ουμε (e.g., γράφουμε, πίνουμε).

sections.title: Most Common Verbs
sections.explanation: Several extremely common Greek verbs are irregular or semi-irregular and must be memorized. είμαι (to be): εγώ είμαι, εσύ είσαι, αυτός/ή/ό είναι, εμείς είμαστε, εσείς είστε/είσαστε, αυτοί/ές/ά είναι. έχω (to have): εγώ έχω, εσύ έχεις, αυτός έχει, εμείς έχουμε, εσείς έχετε, αυτοί έχουν. πάω (to go): εγώ πάω, εσύ πας, αυτός πάει, εμείς πάμε, εσείς πάτε, αυτοί πάνε. κάνω (to do/make): εγώ κάνω, εσύ κάνεις, αυτός κάνει, εμείς κάνουμε, εσείς κάνετε, αυτοί κάνουν. θέλω (to want): εγώ θέλω, εσύ θέλεις, αυτός θέλει, εμείς θέλουμε, εσείς θέλετε, αυτοί θέλουν. λέω (to say): εγώ λέω, εσύ λες, αυτός λέει, εμείς λέμε, εσείς λέτε, αυτοί λένε. Note that είμαι is the most irregular — its forms do not resemble each other, and αυτός and αυτοί share the same form: είναι.
sections.tip: Memorize 'είμαι' first since it appears in almost every sentence. Note that both αυτός (he/she/it) and αυτοί (they) are 'είναι' — this unique feature has no equivalent in most European languages.

examples.el: Αυτός έχει ένα σπίτι στην Αθήνα.
examples.en: He has a house in Athens.
We are going to the beach.
I want a glass of water.
What are you saying? I can't hear you.

exercises.prompt: What is special about 'είναι' in the conjugation of 'είμαι' (to be)?
options: It is only used for αυτός (he)
It is used for both αυτός/ή/ό (he/she/it) and αυτοί/ές/ά (they)
exercises.explanation: Unlike most verbs, 'είμαι' uses the same form 'είναι' for both third person singular (αυτός/ή/ό) and third person plural (αυτοί/ές/ά).

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'We go to the market' in Greek?
exercises.explanation: The εμείς form of 'πάω' (to go) is 'πάμε': Εμείς πάμε στην αγορά. Note: πάτε is the εσείς form and πάει is for αυτός/ή/ό.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct form of 'λέω' (to say) for 'αυτοί' (they)?
exercises.explanation: 'λέω' is irregular: the αυτοί/ές/ά form is 'λένε' (not λέουν). Full conjugation: λέω, λες, λέει, λέμε, λέτε, λένε.
